---
title: "Exercise 1.2.2"
---

## Problem

Show that there is no rational number $r$ satisfying $2^r = 3$.

## Solution

For the sake of contradiction, suppose that there exists $\frac{p}{q} \in \mathbb{Q}$ with
$$
2^{\frac{p}{q}} = 3
$$
where $p,q \in \mathbb{Z}$, $q \not = 0$, and $GCD(p,q) = 1$. 

Clearly, $\frac{p}{q} = 0$ is not a solution here, so let us assume without loss of generality that $p \not = 0$ and $q > 0$.

It follows that
$$
2^p = 3^q
$$
where we can consider the following cases depending on whether $p$ is positive or negative.

Suppose that $p > 0$. It then follows that an even integer $2^p$ is equal to an odd integer $3^q$, which is a contradiction.

Suppose that $p < 0$. It then follows that
$$
2^p = \frac{1}{2^{-p}} = (\frac{1}{2})^{-p}= 3^q
$$
where $(\frac{1}{2})^{-p} < 1$ and $1 < 3^q$, thus $(\frac{1}{2})^{-p} \not = 3^q$, which is a contradiction. 

With contradiction in all cases, it follows that there is no such rational number $r = \frac{p}{q}$ with $2^r = 3$.
